title: Executive Order 11190—Providing for the Screening of the Ready Reserve of the Armed Forces

flags.justification: This delegates the entire screening-regulation-writing authority to the Secretary of Defense with no criteria, categories, or guidance whatsoever, replacing EO 10651's detailed, multi-paragraph criteria (age/fitness standards, hardship provisions, critical-occupation rules, an elected-officials carve-out) with a single unconditioned sentence.
EO 10651 required periodic reports to the President for evaluation against manpower requirements (Sec. 10); this order contains no such requirement at all.

No screening criteria are specified anywhere in this order, a more open-ended standard than even EO 10651's already-flagged 'extreme hardship' language, since at least that order specified some criteria.
No incentive structure distinct from the vagueness already captured under Flag 4.
A standing delegation for an ongoing military-readiness function.

This removes both the periodic-reporting requirement and all specific screening criteria that constrained discretion under its predecessor, EO 10651 — a significant reduction in the accountability structure the prior order had built in.
This concern is captured under Flags 2 and 7.
This substantially increases the Secretary of Defense's unconstrained discretion relative to the detailed predecessor order it revokes, a genuine structural expansion despite the text's brevity.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.

No exemption categories are specified in this order's own text; EO 10651's elected-officials carve-out is not carried forward explicitly, though the Secretary of Defense's unconstrained delegated authority could recreate it by regulation.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 5/14 = 35.7%. This order explicitly revokes EO 10651 (Sec. 2) and replaces its detailed, criteria-based screening framework with a one-sentence blank-check delegation to the Secretary of Defense. It scores higher (35.7%) than its longer, more detailed predecessor (22.2%) — a clean, concrete illustration that textual brevity is not the same thing as good structural design. This is the most severe instance yet of the quiet-regression pattern logged elsewhere in this project (EO 12072, EO 9296): rather than narrowly weakening one provision, it eliminates an entire detailed regulatory framework and its reporting requirement in favor of unconstrained discretion.
